q: A multinational financial firm manages 240 AWS accounts with strict data residency requirements: EU customer data must remain in eu-west-1, Asia-Pacific in ap-southeast-1, and US data in us-east-1. They need centralized security governance while allowing business units self-service account creation. What architecture ensures data residency compliance?
options: Single AWS account with IAM-based regional restrictions and S3 bucket policies
Separate AWS Organizations per region with no cross-organization networking
AWS Organizations with region-restrictive SCPs per OU, Control Tower account factory with region guardrails, and automated Config conformance packs
AWS Config rules that detect non-compliant resources and trigger automatic remediation
explain: AWS Organizations with OUs by region allows SCPs (deny aws:RequestedRegion outside approved) to enforce data sovereignty preventively. Control Tower ensures new accounts inherit guardrails. Config packs verify compliance.

q: An enterprise has 180 AWS accounts across 12 business units with hybrid identity via Active Directory. They need consistent IAM baseline policies, permission boundaries enforcing maximum privilege per role, and audit trails for all cross-account assume-role activity. Which combination is most effective?
options: AWS SSO with identity center, SCPs for baseline, CloudTrail logging
IAM roles in each account with trust relationships to AD
SCPs defining max permissions per OU, permission boundaries at account level, resource-based policies for SSO principals only, organization CloudTrail
explain: SCPs + permission boundaries + CloudTrail provide preventive, permissive, and detective controls combined. Permission boundaries act as hard ceiling regardless of attached policies.

q: Your organization uses AWS Organizations with 250+ accounts spanning 8 OUs. You need to enforce all RDS databases encrypt, all data backed up daily, and all S3 buckets have versioning, with exceptions for sandboxes and dev. What's the most maintainable approach?
options: Control Tower guardrails for preventive controls, Config conformance packs with OU-based exceptions, auto-remediation
SCPs denying unencrypted resources; Config rules with remediation
Service Catalog templates pre-configured for encryption
CloudFormation StackSets deploying Config rules with manual exemptions
explain: Control Tower guardrails are AWS-maintained and versioned. Conformance packs allow exceptions by OU. Config remediation automates fixing. Scales automatically to new accounts.

q: A 200-account AWS environment needs three security domains: Production (PCI-DSS), Protected Health (HIPAA), Development (no compliance). Domains must never allow cross-domain network traffic. Each domain needs isolated billing and compliance reporting. How should OUs be structured?
options: Single OU with tags and Config rules
Three root OUs: Prod-OU, PHI-OU, Dev-OU; SCPs blocking cross-OU assume-role and VPC peering/TGW between OUs; separate billing per domain
Mix of OU hierarchy and tags with SCPs
explain: Three root OUs maintains single billing relationship while SCPs enforce identity and network isolation for regulatory compliance.

q: Your organization has 340 accounts requiring consolidated AWS Bills with chargeback to business units by cost center, project ID, and environment. You need real-time cost visibility and anomaly detection. What's the best architecture?
options: SCPs enforce tagging, then use Cost Explorer
AWS Billing Conductor with custom groups, Cost Explorer, CloudWatch anomaly detector
Cost Allocation Tags via SCPs, Cost and Usage Reports to S3, Athena queries, Lambda anomaly detection
Organizations billing consolidation with detailed CUR to S3, QuickSight dashboards per BU, Compute Optimizer for rightsizing
explain: Organizations consolidates billing automatically. Detailed CUR provides granular data. S3+QuickSight enables self-service BI with full flexibility. Compute Optimizer adds rightsizing.

q: An enterprise is migrating 150 AWS accounts from three unrelated Organizations into a single Organization for unified governance. Legacy Organizations use different tagging, naming, and SCPs. What's the safest sequence?
options: Bulk invite all accounts, then apply Control Tower
Create target OU structure, migrate one-per-BU with tag reconciliation, gradually apply read-only guardrails first, validate with Config packs
Use AWS Migration Accelerator to auto-consolidate
Keep separate, link via AWS RAM
explain: Bulk migration risks breaking workloads. Phased approach: (1) tag reconciliation, (2) per-BU invitation, (3) detective guardrails, (4) validation, (5) preventive SCPs.

q: Your organization operates 200 accounts and needs to implement Control Tower with existing custom CloudFormation guardrails already deployed. Which approach minimizes disruption?
options: Keep CloudFormation, add CT guardrails in detective-only mode
Audit for equivalence, decommission duplicates, import accounts, add CT guardrails for additional coverage
Deploy fresh CT, override custom guardrails
explain: Audit ensures equivalence before decommissioning. Control Tower becomes single source of truth. Gradual decommissioning prevents governance gaps.

q: A healthcare organization with 120 AWS accounts needs HIPAA compliance. Each account processes different patient data. They need per-account audit logs, centralized analysis, tamper-evident logging, and compliance evidence. What's the best architecture?
options: Organization CloudTrail with MFA delete, Object Lock, log file validation, HIPAA conformance pack, Config aggregator
CloudTrail per account to S3 with MFA delete
explain: Organization CloudTrail centralizes with chain-of-custody. Object Lock (read-only) + MFA delete achieves compliance-grade immutability. Log file validation detects tampering. Config aggregator proves compliance.

q: Your organization has 95 accounts across 5 business units with different cost allocation needs and autonomy. CFO needs consolidated billing, departments need independent tracking. Production must have hard isolation from development. What's the optimal structure?
options: One OU per BU with nested prod/dev OUs; use cost tags within each; SCPs limit cross-OU networking
Flat OU with all accounts, use tags
Single prod OU, single dev OU; use tags for BU allocation
explain: Nested OUs mirror organizational structure. Sub-OUs allow different governance for prod (stricter) vs dev (permissive). Cost tags enable fine-grained chargeback.

q: An organization is implementing Service Catalog for self-service provisioning across 50 accounts. Business units provision databases, VPCs, compute. You need to enforce tagging, control costs, and prevent non-approved services. What's the architecture?
options: Central portfolio with approved products, launch constraints with cost tag requirements, SCPs deny non-catalog services, CloudFormation custom resources validate business logic
Portfolio per BU with IAM policies, launch constraints enforcing tags, SCPs blocking non-catalog
No restrictions, rely on Config remediation
explain: Central portfolio maintains consistency. Launch constraints bind specific IAM role. SCPs prevent workarounds. CloudFormation custom resources validate business logic.

q: Your organization has 180 accounts with AWS SSO from Active Directory. You need to ensure all humans assume roles, services use temporary credentials, and cross-account access follows least privilege. How should you architect IAM?
options: SSO for humans with permission sets, IAM roles for services, external IDs for cross-account
SSO groups to permission sets, SCP block IAM users, resource policies for SSO principals only, cross-account roles require MFA
explain: SCP blocks IAM users enforces temporary credentials everywhere. Resource-based policies restrict to SSO principals. MFA on cross-account roles adds security layer.

q: An enterprise with 220 accounts needs to share a VPC Transit Gateway across 15 accounts via RAM. The TGW needs to support hybrid on-premises connectivity via Direct Connect. How should this be architected?
options: TGW in each account with peering
TGW with Direct Connect attachment, share via RAM, each account VPC attaches to TGW with own subnets
TGW in network account, share via RAM, traffic routes through TGW
explain: TGW in network account with DX attachment. AWS RAM shares to 15 accounts automatically. Each account VPCs attach enabling transitive routing.

q: Your organization manages 200+ accounts and needs to enforce compliance: all EC2 instances have detailed monitoring (1-min intervals), all RDS have 30+ day backup retention, all S3 have centralized logging. Which approach is most scalable?
options: Org-wide Config rules with SNS and Lambda
Control Tower guardrails preventive/detective, Config conformance pack with remediation, org S3 bucket for centralized logging with Config aggregator
Service Catalog templates with Config verification
explain: Control Tower guardrails are AWS-maintained. Conformance packs deploy Config rules at org scale. Config remediation automates fixing. Scales to any account count.

q: An organization with 160 accounts enabling CloudTrail organization trail needs: immutable logs, 7-year retention, per-account cost attribution, and <1s forensic query latency. What's the architecture?
options: S3 object lock compliance mode, Athena+Lakeformation
S3 MFA delete + versioning, log file validation, Intelligent-Tiering, CloudTrail Lake for forensics, S3 Storage Lens for cost attribution
S3 object lock governance, lifecycle to Glacier, Athena queries
Per-account trails with replication to central
explain: MFA delete + versioning prevents accidental deletion. CloudTrail Lake provides sub-second query latency. Intelligent-Tiering optimizes 7-year costs. Storage Lens attributes costs.

q: Your organization has 3-tier OU hierarchy (Root > Division > Department > Team) with 250+ accounts. Different tiers need different governance. How should SCPs be structured?
options: Single set at root affecting all
SCPs at each level: root (baseline security), division (residency), department (cost controls), team (service restrictions); union inheritance
explain: Hierarchical SCPs apply union-of-denies: root denies risky services, division denies non-approved regions, department denies expensive services, team denies specific services. Scalable per tier.

q: An organization consolidating 5 separate AWS accounts into single Organization has existing CloudTrail trails in 2 accounts and custom Config rules across all. What's the safest consolidation?
options: Create org trail parallel to account trails, validate 2 weeks, disable account trails; migrate Config rules to packs gradually via aggregator
Immediate org trail, disable account trails
explain: Parallel validation proves org trail works. Gradual Config migration prevents governance gaps. Organization trail eventually provides single source of truth.

q: Your 140-account organization needs encryption for all data stores (RDS, DynamoDB, S3, EBS). BUs have different key needs: AWS-managed, customer-managed, or HSM. How should this be implemented?
options: Per-account customer-managed keys; central account for HSM; Config detecting non-compliance
Per-OU SCPs per BU strategy; per-account keys; CT guardrails; Config with remediation
Single KMS key in central account via RAM
explain: Per-OU SCPs allow different strategies (AWS-managed vs customer-managed vs HSM) based on BU needs. Per-account KMS provides isolation. Control Tower + Config ensures compliance.

q: Your organization manages 200+ accounts with Firewall Manager for org-wide firewall policies (WAF, security groups, network firewalls). Some accounts need stricter rules. How should policies be structured?
options: Single policy set org-wide with Config exemptions
Policies per security domain (dev, prod, sensitive), applied via scope targeting OUs; exclusion list for exceptions; local policies extend (not override)
explain: Firewall Manager policy scope enables domain-specific policies. Exclusion lists exempt dev accounts. Local policies extend (additive) preventing security gaps.

q: Your organization operates 250 accounts across 3 continents with GDPR residency. Requirements: data stays in-region, keys stay in-region, compliance audits per-region, global consolidated billing. What's the architecture?
options: Single Orgs at global, EU/APAC/Americas as regional OUs, regional SCPs, regional keys, per-region Config aggregators
Accounts pinned to regions via SCP, global key
Separate Orgs per region with RAM federation
explain: Single Organizations with regional OUs + SCPs achieves residency enforcement while maintaining consolidated billing and central governance.

q: An organization with 95 accounts implementing RBAC via AWS SSO has custom permission sets per account, but 30% of permissions are identical. How should this be optimized?
options: Create shared baseline permission set for all accounts, account-specific permission sets stack on top
Use IAM Identity Center policy inheritance
Union all permissions in each set
explain: AWS SSO allows multiple permission sets assigned to same user in single account. Shared baseline + account-specific stacking avoids 30% duplication.

q: A 180-account organization implementing Service Catalog for provisioning must prevent shadow IT. You must block direct resource creation while allowing catalog use. What's the best approach?
options: SCPs block IAM user creation; launch constraints bind specific role; resource policies allow only that role; Config detects non-catalog with remediation
SCPs deny all creation except catalog role
explain: SCP blocks IAM users, launch constraints bind role, resource policies restrict access, Config remediation terminates non-catalog resources. Prevents all shadow IT paths.

q: Your organization manages 300+ accounts enforcing complex compliance for sensitive data (PII, PHI, payment cards). Requirements: encryption, logging, exfiltration monitoring. What's the best architecture?
options: Macie for discovery/classification, org CloudTrail, immutable S3, GuardDuty for threat detection, SIEM via EventBridge
DLP via StackSets, per-account encryption SCPs, manual aggregation
S3 Block Public Access, per-account CloudTrail, local Config, manual reviews
Encrypted S3, access logging, CloudWatch alarms
explain: Macie discovers data, GuardDuty detects exfiltration patterns, EventBridge streams to SIEM for real-time response. Provides full visibility and threat detection.

q: An organization with 200 accounts needs to comply with requirement: no AWS secrets in code or environment variables. What's the best enforced architecture?
options: Parameter Store for config, Secrets Manager for secrets
Code review policies and SSO access
SCPs block IAM users, Secrets Manager for all secrets, resource policies restrict to service roles, CodeBuild pulls secrets at build time, CloudTrail logs access
explain: SCP prevents long-term credentials. Secrets Manager stores all secrets. Resource policies grant access only to specific roles. CodeBuild retrieves at build time. Technologically enforced.

q: Your 120-account organization implementing auto-remediation for compliance violations. An S3 bucket created without encryption. What's the safest automated remediation approach?
options: Config detects, Lambda enables encryption, SNS notifies team for approval, implements after confirmation
Quarantine bucket with SCPs, manual remediation required
Lambda enables immediately, CloudTrail audit, manual review after
explain: Auto-detection + enable + manual approval prevents accidentally breaking changes (e.g., breaking dev use cases) while maintaining automation benefits.

q: A 170-account organization needs to enforce MFA for all human access to production accounts. Teams have different MFA requirements (TOTP, hardware keys, Okta). How to implement?
options: Okta + SSO integration, permission sets with MFA conditions
SCPs deny assume-role without MFA, SSO with MFA, per-account IAM roles
Production OUs with SCPs denying assume-role without aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent, SSO with MFA, KMS-encrypted MFA seeds, CloudTrail verification
IAM policies requiring MFA in prod accounts
explain: SCP denies assume-role without MFA (preventive). SSO enforces MFA at identity level. KMS encrypts MFA seeds. CloudTrail audits MFA usage. Ensures no human bypasses MFA.

q: Your 250-account organization needs least-privilege networking with VPCs in 5 regions. All inter-account traffic routes through centralized hubs per region, all internet traffic through egress firewalls. What's the architecture?
options: TGW per region with DX, cross-region TGW peering, centralized egress via network firewall
TGW per OU with cascading peering
explain: TGW per region scales to 5000+ attachments. Network firewall provides centralized DLP inspection. TGW peering enables cross-region routing efficiently.

q: Your organization manages 200+ accounts auditing 500+ GB/day CloudTrail logs. Needs: <1s forensic query latency, per-account cost attribution, 7-year compliance retention. What architecture meets requirements?
options: S3 + CloudFront, Glacier after 30 days, Athena analysis
S3 partitioned by account/date, Intelligent-Tiering, CloudTrail Lake for forensics, S3 Storage Lens for attribution
CloudWatch Logs, Insights queries, 30-day default retention
explain: CloudTrail Lake provides sub-second query latency. Intelligent-Tiering optimizes 7-year costs. S3 Storage Lens provides per-account attribution.

q: A 160-account organization implementing change management requires all infrastructure changes through approval process before deployment. What's the enforcement architecture?
options: All infrastructure via CloudFormation with peer review
CodePipeline with approval stages, CodeDeploy to members
Service Catalog with approval workflows, SCPs block direct creation, Config rules enforce
SCPs block CloudFormation except via approved system
explain: Service Catalog provides approval workflows. SCPs prevent direct creation. Config rules detect non-catalog resources. Ensures all changes go through approval.

q: Your organization has 180 accounts implementing chargeback with multi-dimensional allocation: business units, project IDs, teams, cost centers. Different services have different rules (compute per team, storage per BU). How to architect?
options: Enforce tags, CUR to S3, Athena + Redshift allocation, per-OU chargeback
Enforce 4 tags via SCPs, use Cost Allocation in billing, Cost Explorer grouping
Organizations + SCPs, detailed CUR to S3, Lambda cost engine with service-specific rules, cost reports to FinOps
explain: Lambda cost allocation engine applies service-specific rules that built-in tools can't. Cost and Usage Report has raw data for custom logic at scale.

q: A 200-account organization implementing Lake Formation for data lake serving 5 BUs with different access. Some data restricted to units, some shared. How to design?
options: Single catalog, database per BU, table permissions per unit
Central account with RAM, databases per BU in S3, IAM-based access
Central Lake Formation catalog, databases per BU, table-level permissions via cross-account roles, shared data accessible via permissions
explain: Centralized Lake Formation with cross-account roles enables fine-grained table-level access. Shared databases accessible to multiple BUs via resource permissions.

q: Your 140-account organization runs hybrid with on-premises DCs via multiple DX connections. HA and failover needed across 3 AWS regions. How should this be architected?
options: Single 10 Gbps DX with automatic routing
Redundant DX per region (min 2 each), different edge locations, BGP AS_PATH prepending, TGW per region with peering, on-prem BGP peers all DX
Two DX (1 Gbps each) to primary for redundancy, VGW peering on-prem failover
Single DX to primary, Route 53 failover to secondaries
explain: Multiple DX per region with BGP AS_PATH prepending provides active-active with automatic failover. TGW peering enables cross-region routing.

q: An organization with 210 accounts implementing AppConfig for distributed configuration management. Different teams need different configs (feature flags, endpoints, timeouts). How should AppConfig be structured?
options: Centralized in management account, cross-account RAM access, applications per BU, IAM roles per team
Applications per BU, environments per team/region, profiles per service, IAM roles restrict team access
Single application with environments per team
explain: Centralized AppConfig via AWS RAM enables cross-account access with fine-grained IAM-based restrictions per team. Single source of truth for configurations.

q: A 180-account healthcare organization implementing HIPAA audit logging needs: all API calls logged, logs immutable, compliance team only access for 6+ years, quarterly reports, encryption proof. What's the architecture?
options: Organization CloudTrail, S3 Object Lock (compliance mode), KMS encryption in compliance team's account, log file validation, quarterly reports via Config, access logging
CloudTrail to S3 with MFA delete, lifecycle to Glacier
explain: Object Lock compliance mode provides immutability. KMS in compliance team's account proves key ownership. Log file validation detects tampering. Access logging proves controls. Quarterly reports prove compliance.

q: Your 165-account organization implementing automated incident response: GuardDuty detects threat, system should isolate resource, preserve logs, alert team. What's the architecture?
options: Org GuardDuty with delegated admin, findings → EventBridge → Step Functions: Lambda isolates (revoke creds, remove SG), EBS snapshot for forensics, SNS alerts, Systems Manager collects logs
GuardDuty → CloudWatch Events → Lambda isolates resource, SNS alerts
GuardDuty per account with local automation
explain: Org GuardDuty + EventBridge + Step Functions orchestrates multi-step response while preserving forensic evidence. Systems Manager ensures log collection.

q: A 195-account organization implementing cross-account backup/DR for production databases (RDS, DynamoDB) in 20 primary accounts to central DR account with automatic failover capability. How to architect?
options: AWS Backup with copy rules to central DR, cross-account IAM roles, automated DynamoDB on-demand backups with replication, pre-configured restore role in DR
Backup agent per account, centralized backup server
Local RDS backups, manual copy to DR, manual RDS replica promotion
RDS read replicas in DR, Global Tables for DynamoDB
explain: AWS Backup copy rules automate replication. Cross-account roles enable service execution. Pre-configured restore role minimizes RTO. Handles heterogeneous DB types.

q: Your 200-account organization needs to enforce data classification and prevent exfiltration. All data classified (public, internal, confidential, restricted), attempts blocked/alerted. What's the architecture?
options: Macie for discovery/classification, SCPs block S3 GetObject on unclassified, GuardDuty detects bulk downloads, DLP Gateway blocks network exfil
Manual classification, Config enforcement, Lambda remediation
Macie tagging, IAM policies per classification
Macie for classification, S3 bucket policies block unclassified, Config detects
explain: Macie discovers data (prevention), GuardDuty detects exfiltration patterns (detection), DLP Gateway blocks at network level (response). Defense-in-depth approach.

q: A 220-account organization consolidating VPCs from multiple legacy environments into hub-and-spoke. 50 legacy VPCs across regions with different IP ranges and security group configs. What's the safest approach?
options: Create new VPCs, migrate apps via database tools
VPC peering interim, plan TGW migration later
Bulk migrate all VPCs to TGW, test after cutover
Create TGW infrastructure, validate 5 pilot VPCs for 2 weeks, waves of 20 VPCs/week with pre-flight validation (IP overlap, SG audit), post-migration testing, rollback per wave
explain: Phased migration with pilot validation and per-wave rollback plans minimizes blast radius. Pre-flight validation prevents IP conflicts and security issues.

q: Your organization has 240 accounts implementing cross-account backup and recovery for Kubernetes (EKS) clusters with 20 primary clusters. What's the architecture?
options: EKS etcd snapshots in account, no cross-account
Velero deployed in each cluster, snapshots to central S3 via cross-account role, cross-account restore role pre-configured in DR
AWS Backup for EKS with central account
Container image scanning, registry replication, assume stateless
explain: Velero captures cluster state, persistent volumes, and application config. Cross-account role enables rapid recovery. Standard Kubernetes backup approach.

q: A 175-account organization implementing encryption key management needs: customer-managed KMS in each account for isolation, but centralized key rotation/audit from security account, and DR keys. How to architect?
options: Customer-managed KMS per account, all trust security account root
Multi-region KMS per account with auto-rotation, security account read-only audit role (GetKeyPolicy, ListKeys), central CloudTrail
Customer-managed KMS per account replicated to security for DR, assume key admin role for rotation
HSM-backed keys in central cluster via PrivateLink
explain: Multi-region KMS + automatic rotation + read-only audit role achieves centralized key management without granting admin access.

q: A 205-account organization implementing Trusted Advisor checks org-wide with centralized reporting and remediation recommendations. What's the architecture?
options: Organization Support plan with API refresh, findings → S3 → Athena queries, QuickSight dashboard per OU
explain: Organizations Support plan enables API refresh across accounts. S3 + Athena + QuickSight provides scalable analysis at 200+ account level.

q: Your 180-account organization implementing Systems Manager Fleet Manager for managing 10,000+ EC2 instances across accounts and on-premises. What's the architecture?
options: Central Systems Manager as aggregator, cross-account IAM roles, central patching via SSM documents, hybrid activation for on-prem, Patch Manager compliance dashboard
Session Manager per account with local patching
Independent per account with manual sync
Automation per account with local workflows
explain: Central Systems Manager with cross-account IAM roles aggregates fleet. Hybrid Activation includes on-premises servers. Patch Manager provides central compliance visibility.

q: Your 185-account organization adopting AWS Outposts for on-premises infrastructure. How to integrate into multi-account Organizations architecture?
options: Outposts in Organizations with own OU, TGW on-prem connection, hybrid deployment via IaC, SSO extending to Outposts
Separate AWS account via Direct Connect
explain: Outposts in Organizations with TGW on-premises connection enables consistent hybrid governance, billing, and unified infrastructure as code.

q: A 200-account financial organization implementing FinSpace for financial data management. Different BUs have different regulatory requirements. How to architect?
options: Single account, BUs access via API with tags
Single environment, data partitioned per BU via IAM
Separate FinSpace per BU for isolation
Dataset per BU with cross-BU sharing via FinSpace permissions, centralized ingestion via Kinesis/S3, BU workspaces with restricted access, cross-account IAM roles
explain: FinSpace dataset per BU with fine-grained permissions enables regulatory isolation while allowing controlled sharing between units.

q: Your 195-account organization migrating from on-premises CA-signed certificates to AWS ACM. 5,000+ services depend on these certificates. How to manage migration?
options: Import per account for isolation with sync
Bulk import CA certs to ACM in primary account
Import CA key to ACM in security account, cross-account IAM roles, enable auto-renewal, migrate services in 200/week waves with validation, dual-cert setup during transition
explain: Phased migration with dual-cert setup and per-wave validation minimizes outage risk for 5000+ services depending on certs.

q: A 205-account organization implementing continuous Config compliance monitoring. Configuration drift must be detected within 1 hour. What's the architecture?
options: Continuous Config recording, per-account analysis, quarterly reviews
Org-wide Config via Control Tower with 1-hour recorder frequency, multi-region aggregator, conformance packs, non-compliant → EventBridge → SNS alerts and Systems Manager remediation
Config per account with central aggregator
explain: 1-hour recorder + conformance packs + EventBridge automation detects and remediates drift within 1-hour target window automatically.

q: Your organization with 310 accounts needs sub-second cost attribution for 500+ services. Different business models apply per service (consumption, reserved capacity, hybrid). How to architect?
options: Billing Conductor with service-specific groups and custom pricing
Detailed CUR partitioned by service, Redshift data warehouse with dimensional schema (service, BU, account, time), BI tools with row-level security
Cost Allocation Tags with Cost Explorer grouping
S3 bucketing by cost center, Athena cost analysis
explain: Redshift dimensional schema enables complex cost allocation queries that CUR export + Athena cannot support. Row-level security isolates BUs' cost data.

q: A 280-account organization implementing Grafana across hybrid (AWS + on-premises). Different teams need visibility into own resources only. Central team needs org-wide view. How to architect?
options: CloudWatch agent on all instances, export to Grafana via API
Per-team Grafana instances with data syncing
Central Grafana EC2 with SSH access per team
Grafana Cloud for managed service, AWS data source with cross-account IAM roles, on-prem agents send metrics, team folders with RBAC
explain: Grafana Cloud managed service scales automatically. Cross-account IAM roles aggregate AWS data. Team folders with RBAC provide isolation while central dashboard shows enterprise view.

q: Your 225-account organization implements automated account decommissioning: old accounts must be deleted, data archived 7 years, billing consolidated. Process involves 50+ account deletions monthly. How to automate?
options: AWS Batch jobs for data export to central S3, Config compliance check, close account via AWS Organizations API
Manual AWS account suspension, data export
Automation account with Systems Manager, scheduled daily: identify deletable accounts (via tag), backup via AWS Backup, clean resources per deletion SCP, close via org API
explain: Scheduled Systems Manager automation with Config compliance checks ensures safe, consistent deletion. Central S3 archive enables 7-year retention. No manual operator required.

q: A 190-account organization with 15 teams implementing Spot Fleet for HPC workloads. Different teams have different cost targets and capacity needs. How to manage?
options: Per-team Spot Fleet with capacity reservations per team, allocation strategy (lowest-price vs diversified), AWS Batch for job queueing, cost tracking per team via tags
Single Spot Fleet shared by all teams
explain: Per-team Spot Fleets with capacity reservations ensure isolation. AWS Batch manages queue globally. Diversified allocation reduces Spot interruption risk for critical jobs.

q: Your 200-account organization implementing multi-region disaster recovery for databases. RPO must be <1 minute, RTO <15 minutes. 150 databases across 3 regions. How to architect?
options: Aurora Global Database for MySQL/PostgreSQL (RPO <1s), DMS continuous replication for others, Route 53 health check → Lambda promotes read replica, Systems Manager activation for data validation
Read replicas per region, Route 53 health checks, manual promotion
explain: Aurora Global DB achieves RPO <1s natively. DMS continuous replication handles others. Lambda + Route 53 enables automated <15min RTO promotion process.

q: A 240-account organization implementing LakeFormation for PII data governance. PII must not be accessed outside 2 principal geographies. How to enforce?
options: Lake Formation table permissions via cross-account roles with geo-restriction tags, Macie + EventBridge block non-geo access attempts, log table access
Databases per geography, manual access review
S3 bucket policy block public access
explain: Lake Formation table-level permissions via geo-tagged roles. Macie detects PII access from outside permitted geos. EventBridge alerts and blocks.

q: Your 170-account organization implements DMS (Database Migration Service) to migrate 80 Oracle on-premises databases to Aurora on AWS. Minimal downtime (<5 mins), 24/7 operation, current production load 10 Gbps. What's the architecture?
options: AWS DataSync with custom Oracle export
Single DMS replication instance, serial migration

Dedicated DMS task per database, parallel instances, continuous replication weeks prior, LOB chunking for large migrations, CDC during final cutover with brief app pause, validation per database before committing
explain: Parallel DMS tasks handle 80 databases concurrently. Weeks of continuous replication enables brief final cutover. CDC ensures consistency during cutover moment.

q: A 185-account healthcare organization implementing SFTP gateway on AWS for on-premises partner data exchanges. HIPAA requires encryption in transit and at rest, audit trails, per-partner access control, weekly rotation of credentials. How to architect?
options: AWS Transfer Family (SFTP) in VPC with private ELB, LDAP auth to on-premises AD, S3 backend with encryption+versioning, CloudTrail logging all transfers, automated credential rotation via Secrets Manager + Lambda
EC2 running OpenSSH with user accounts
explain: AWS Transfer Family provides managed SFTP. LDAP auth integrates with existing AD. S3 encryption+versioning meets HIPAA. Secrets Manager + Lambda automates credential rotation.

q: Your 210-account organization managing 50,000+ EC2 instances implements automated patching across all accounts and regions. Windows & Linux, monthly patches, 99.95% uptime requirement during patching window. How to architect?
options: Run Command per account, manual batching

Systems Manager Patch Manager with cross-account role, maintenance windows per region (staggered), auto-reboot with health check groups (ASG, NLB target deregistration), rollback groups if errors detected
EC2 ImageBuilder for automated AMI patching
explain: Systems Manager Patch Manager with health check groups enables rolling updates maintaining uptime. Staggered windows across regions distribute load. Auto-reboot with target deregistration ensures graceful updates.

q: A 220-account organization implementing AppStream 2.0 for 5,000+ distributed workforce to access on-premises applications without VPN. Low-latency is critical. How to architect?
options: Desktop AMI shared via Systems Manager Session Manager
Single AppStream 2.0 fleet in primary region

Regional fleets in 4 major regions with connection preference based on latency, hybrid gateway for on-premises app routing via Direct Connect, cross-region user/fleet profile replication, load balancing via Route 53 latency policy
explain: Regional fleets with latency routing enable low-latency access. Hybrid gateway routes to on-premises. Profile replication ensures user experience consistency across regions.

q: Your 200-account organization implements EventBridge to orchestrate 500+ microservices across AWS and on-premises. Services have variable latency (10ms to 5s). How to ensure reliable event delivery and prevent cascading failures?
options: EventBridge with Dead-Letter Queues per service, exponential backoff, Rule-based routing with retry policies (max 2 hours), Circuit breaker patterns via Lambda, monitoring via X-Ray tracing
SNS for pub/sub, per-service retry logic
explain: DLQ + exponential backoff + time limits prevent message loss. Rule-based retry policies vary by service latency. X-Ray tracks failures across hybrid infrastructure.

q: A 195-account organization implements KMS key management with cross-account key sharing for encryption. 500+ keys across accounts. Key audit and rotation must be centralized, but deletion requires approval from account owner. How to architect?
options: Keys in each account, S3 replication for backup

Keys per account, central CloudTrail for audit, cross-account assume role for rotation, per-account approval for deletion via Lambda approval gate, KMS key policy allows both account and central key admin
explain: Per-account keys provide isolation. Cross-account assume role enables central rotation. Lambda approval gate honors deletion approval from account owner. Prevents rogue central admin from deleting keys.

q: Your 230-account organization implements Secrets Manager with 10,000+ secrets. Audit team must see secret names and rotation status (never values), developers access only their app's secrets, emergency access via approval gate. How to architect?
options: Parameter Store for non-sensitive, Secrets Manager for sensitive
Secrets per account, IAM policies per developer
Central account with secrets for all teams
Secrets Manager cross-account resource policy, resource-based policies by secret (read-only for audit, read-only-values for devs), approval role for emergency access, CloudTrail logs all access
explain: Resource-based policies enable fine-grained access (audit sees metadata, devs see values, approval gate for emergency). CloudTrail provides audit trail. Prevents privilege escalation via assume-role.

q: A 175-account organization implementing AWS Marketplace for software procurement. Different BUs need independent Marketplace subscriptions, but central finance wants consolidated billing and license tracking. How to architect?
options: Single central account subscription, distribute via API calls
Marketplace subscriptions per BU account, bulk billing

Marketplace subscriptions in member accounts with linked payment method to master account, subscription tracking tag + Config rule validation, cost allocation report per BU, quarterly renewal approval via Approval Group
explain: Marketplace subscriptions in member accounts enable BU independence, linked payment enables consolidated billing, Config rules track subscriptions, Approval Group centralizes renewal.

q: Your 205-account organization running Kubernetes (EKS) needs multi-cluster orchestration for resilience. 15 clusters across 3 regions. Cross-cluster load balancing, automated failover, state replication. How to architect?
options: Per-cluster Helm deployments with manual sync
Single cluster across regions via EKS Anywhere

EKS on EC2 + Fargate, Consul service mesh for cross-cluster service discovery, Global Accelerator for traffic distribution, EBS snapshots + Velero for state replication across clusters, RTO <5min failover
explain: Consul provides distributed service mesh for cross-cluster discovery. Global Accelerator distributes traffic with health checks. Velero + EBS snapshots replicate application state.

q: A 190-account organization implementing Compliance Manager for audits (SOC 2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA). 200+ requirements, different accounts have different compliance scope. How to aggregate and report?
options: Security Hub standard + Compliance Manager with scope filtering per control, Config aggregator for org-wide compliance score, automated reporting to audit team via QuickSight, exceptions via approval workflow
Config rules per requirement, per-account audit
explain: Security Hub + Compliance Manager aggregates. Config aggregator provides org-wide view. QuickSight enables automated reporting. Approval workflow tracks exceptions.

q: Your 215-account organization implements cost anomaly detection. Anomalies must trigger <5 min alerts to team, auto-remediation for known patterns, escalation for novel patterns. How to architect?
options: Cost anomaly detector with SNS notifications
Cost Explorer trending alerts, manual review

Cost Anomaly Detector rule per cost dimension (service, region, account), EventBridge rule routes alerts to Lambda (for auto-remediation of known patterns) and PagerDuty (for novel patterns), Cost Optimizer recommendations auto-applied if pattern recognized from prior incidents
explain: EventBridge + Lambda enables conditional routing: known patterns auto-remediate, novel patterns escalate. Cost Optimizer recommendations applied only for recognized patterns minimizes risk.

q: A 200-account organization implements Amazon MWAA (Managed Apache Airflow) for ETL/data pipeline orchestration across 8 BUs. Each BU needs isolated environments, but shared metadata database and monitoring. How to architect?
options: MWAA environment per BU in member accounts, cross-account RDS for shared metadata, cross-account S3 logs for central monitoring, CloudWatch log group with cross-account role, IAM cross-account permissions for operators
Single MWAA environment, DAGs per BU
Glue jobs per BU instead of Airflow
explain: MWAA per BU provides isolation and cost allocation. Shared RDS metadata enables coordination. Cross-account logs enable central monitoring without centralizing all environments.

q: Your 225-account organization implements automated resource tagging compliance. All resources must have 5 tags (CostCenter, Owner, Environment, Service, DataClass). Non-compliant resources must be auto-tagged within 24 hours or terminated. How to enforce?
options: IAM policies require tagging at user level
Config rules with SNS alerts, manual tagging

SCPs require tag keys at creation time (with deny for missing tags), Config non-compliance triggers Lambda that tags missing-tag resources (infers from resource properties, account tags, or defaults), 24h remediation window, Config compliance dashboard
explain: SCPs prevent creation without tags preventively. Lambda auto-tags within 24h window respecting human review (infers intelligently). Config dashboard shows progress toward compliance.

q: A 185-account organization implementing least-privilege database access. DBA team needs emergent access to prod databases without standing permissions. Auditor needs to see what accessed what. How to architect for PostgreSQL on RDS?
options: VPN access to RDS endpoint, database user per engineer
Master credentials shared with DBA team
RDS Proxy with IAM authentication, temp IAM database auth tokens, RDS proxy logs SQL queries, Systems Manager Session Manager for DBA console access with approval, CloudTrail logs assumed roles
Database user per team member, manual grant/revoke
explain: RDS Proxy + IAM auth enables temporary credentials scoped to tokens with TTL. Session Manager + approval gate controls access. Query logging + CloudTrail provide audit trail.

q: Your 240-account organization manages 15,000+ Lambda functions across all accounts. Deployment must be automated, consistent across accounts, and auditable. Any function version change must log who, when, what. How to architect?
options: CodePipeline with cross-account deployment role, SAM/CloudFormation for IaC, CodeBuild tests per account, automated rollback on alarm, CloudTrail logs all deployments, per-version Lambda environment variable logs change requestor and timestamp
S3 deployment zip per function, per-account Lambda execution
explain: CodePipeline + CodeBuild + SAM ensures consistent, testable deployments. Cross-account role enables central orchestration. CloudTrail + environment variables provide complete audit trail of changes.

q: A 195-account organization implements automated backup and restore testing for RDS databases across all accounts. 500+ databases. Restore testing must occur weekly without impacting production, generate test data report. How to architect?
options: AWS Backup per account, manual testing

AWS Backup with cross-account backup vault, Systems Manager automation weekly: restore snapshot to test account → data validation Lambda → drop test DB, generates test report per database, SNS alerts on failures
CloudFormation snapshots with per-week Lambda execution
explain: AWS Backup centralizes backup management. Systems Manager automation orchestrates weekly testing in isolated test account. Lambda validation + reporting ensures RTO/RPO verification.

q: Your organization needs to interconnect 12 AWS regions with consistent, low-latency multi-region routing. 5,000+ services across regions, dynamic scaling. What's the most scalable architecture?
options: Transit Gateway in each region with multi-region peering, dynamic routing protocol (BGP), shared services in secondary regions via Resource Access Manager
explain: TGW per region + multi-region peering scales to thousands of attachments per region. BGP enables dynamic routing for failover. RAM shares resources across regions automatically.

q: A financial services firm needs active-active multi-region deployment with RPO = 0 (zero data loss). Customers expect <100ms latency globally. What architecture supports this?
options: DynamoDB Global Tables for real-time sync, Aurora Global Database for SQL databases, Route 53 geolocation routing, application state in DynamoDB (strong consistency within region, eventual across regions)
S3 cross-region replication with Route 53 failover
Batch replication every hour to secondaries
explain: DynamoDB Global Tables replicate sub-100ms across regions. Aurora Global DB routes writes to primary, reads from any region. Route 53 geolocation directs users to nearest region. RPO = 0 achieved.

q: Your organization runs hybrid cloud: on-premises VMware 10,000+ VMs, needs quick migration to AWS. VMs must remain on-premises during pilot. How to architect?
options: VMware Cloud on AWS: consistent hypervisor on-prem and cloud, vCenter extends to AWS, VMotion to cloud for zero-downtime migration, HCX acceleration for 100s of VMs/day migration throughput
Convert VMs to AMI via EC2 ImageBuilder
AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) only after commitment
explain: VMware Cloud on AWS maintains VMware consistency. HCX enables rapid bulk migration. VMotion provides zero-downtime moves to pilot cloud infrastructure.

q: Your organization has 50+ petabytes of data in on-premises data center needing migration to S3 data lake. Network bandwidth limited to 100 Mbps. Timeline: 6 months. What's the best approach?
options: S3 Transfer Acceleration with HTTP connections
AWS Snowball Edge 100TB devices with on-prem cluster, 60-day round-trip, parallel imports to S3, ~840 devices for 50PB transfers
explain: Snowball Edge 100TB devices enable physical transfer of massive data volumes. Parallel imports allow 50PB in 6-month timeline at 100Mbps. Cost-effective vs 10Gbps DX for one-time migration.

q: Your organization needs a data lake serving 50 concurrent data scientists with sub-second query latency on 100TB dataset. SQL + ML workloads. Low operational overhead. What's the architecture?
options: Lake Formation + Redshift Spectrum for SQL (parquet optimized format), SageMaker for ML access same Lake Formation catalog, DW nodes for sub-second latency on dimension tables, Athena for exploratory queries
explain: Redshift Spectrum queries S3 data. Redshift DW nodes provide sub-second queries on fact tables. SageMaker uses Lake Formation catalog. Athena for ad-hoc exploration. Unified metadata via Lake Formation.

q: Your organization operates API platform serving 100,000+ requests/second. APIs use different patterns: REST (70%), gRPC (20%), GraphQL (10%). Need unified API management, rate limiting per customer, monetization. How to architect?
options: API Gateway for REST/GraphQL (JSON transformation), Network Load Balancer for gRPC services, API Gateway Usage Plans for rate limiting, CloudFormation templates for consistent deployment, backend services independently scaled
Three separate API services per protocol
explain: API Gateway handles REST/GraphQL. NLB handles gRPC (binary protocol). API Gateway Usage Plans enforce rate limiting and monetization. Independent scaling per service type.

q: Your organization requires complex API authentication: OAuth 2.0 for web clients, SAML for enterprise, mTLS for service-to-service, mutual TLS for partner integrations. Single API endpoint. How to architect?
options: API Gateway authorizer supporting all protocols

CloudFront with Lambda@Edge for protocol switching, API Gateway with multiple authorizers (Lambda for OAuth, Cognito for SAML, request/response transform), backend mutual TLS via ALB listener rules, partner mTLS via ACM client certificates
explain: Lambda@Edge routes based on client fingerprint. API Gateway supports multiple authorizers. ALB listener rules route based on TLS cert. Enables single endpoint with protocol-specific auth.

q: Your organization runs real-time event streaming: 1M events/second, 100+ consumer groups, 30-day retention, sub-second latency delivery. Consumers are AWS services and external partners via HTTPS. What's the architecture?
options: Kinesis Data Streams with on-demand pricing (auto-scales to 1M EPS), Kinesis Firehose to S3 for 30-day archive, Lambda consumers for AWS services, HTTP endpoint for partners with delivery streams → Lambda transformer → HTTPS endpoint
SNS topics with SQS queues per consumer
explain: Kinesis on-demand auto-scales to 1M EPS. Firehose handles archive and fanout to partners. Lambda enables AWS service integration. HTTP endpoint enables external partner consumption.

q: Your organization implements IoT solution: 10M connected devices sending sensor data, event-driven processing (anomalies trigger alerts <1s), device management, fleet updates. How to architect?
options: IoT Core with MQTT/HTTPS protocols, rules engine routes to services (Lambda for alerts, DynamoDB for state, S3 for archives), IoT Fleet Provisioning for scalable device onboarding, IoT Jobs for OTA firmware updates, SDK on devices, TLS cert-based auth
explain: IoT Core manages 10M devices natively with MQTT. Rules engine enables serverless event routing. Fleet Provisioning auto-scales device onboarding. Jobs enable zero-touch firmware updates.

q: Your organization needs a content delivery network for global applications. Primary concern: latency (users within 10ms of edge), security (DDoS mitigation, bot detection), custom origin logic (weighted routing to origins). How to architect?
options: CloudFront with Shield Standard (DDoS), Web ACL for bot detection + WAF, origin group (primary + secondary with auto-failover), Lambda@Edge for custom origin routing logic based on user geo/time/header, caching optimization per content type
explain: CloudFront + Shield + WAF provides defense-in-depth. Origin groups enable transparent failover. Lambda@Edge enables custom routing logic at edge without touching origin. 200+ edges minimize latency.

q: Your organization runs microservices (500+ services) with complex inter-service communication. Services have different SLAs (99.9%, 99.99%, 99.999%). Traffic routing must respect service SLAs. How to architect?
options: AWS App Mesh with Envoy sidecars, Traffic policy per service pair (retry count, timeout, circuit breaker based on SLA), X-Ray tracing for observability, CloudWatch alarms per SLA target, auto-scaling groups per SLA tier
Direct service-to-service calls with client-side retry logic
API Gateway for all inter-service calls
explain: App Mesh enables traffic policy per service pair respecting SLAs. Envoy sidecars provide transparent retry/timeout/circuit-breaker. X-Ray traces failures. Auto-scaling groups dedicate capacity per SLA tier.

q: Your organization has petabyte-scale data warehouse. BI team runs complex aggregation queries (OLAP). Data scientists run machine learning on same data. Workloads have different resource needs and latency SLAs. How to architect?
options: Separate Redshift + separate EMR for ML
Redshift Spectrum to S3 for both workloads
Single Redshift cluster with mixed workloads

Redshift cluster for OLAP queries with materialized views, SageMaker for ML (direct access to Redshift or S3 via Redshift Spectrum), separate compute queues for BI vs ML to prevent resource contention, RLS (Row-level Security) per user/team
explain: Separate compute queues within single cluster prevent BI/ML resource contention. Materialized views optimize BI queries. Spectrum enables ML on broader dataset. RLS enforces data access per user.

q: Your organization implements serverless application: Lambda functions, DynamoDB for storage, API Gateway for APIs, Step Functions for orchestration. Functions are latency-sensitive (P99 < 100ms). How to optimize?
options: Lambda provisioned concurrency (eliminates cold start), VPC endpoint for DynamoDB (eliminates NAT latency), DynamoDB on-demand with DAX caching layer for sub-millisecond reads, Step Functions Express workflows for short-lived executions
Lambda in VPC with ENI allocation
explain: Provisioned concurrency eliminates cold starts. VPC endpoint removes NAT overhead. DAX provides sub-ms caching. Express workflows scale instantly. Achieves P99 < 100ms target.

q: Your organization runs batch data processing: 100TB input, 50 billion operations, distributed computation. Jobs run overnight with <4 hour budget. How to architect for cost-efficiency?
options: Lambda with SQS for distributed processing
AWS Batch with Spot Fleet (80% cost savings), job definition with retry logic for Spot interruptions, dependency DAG via job dependencies, S3 input/output with multipart transfer optimization
explain: Batch + Spot Fleet saves 80% vs on-demand. Job dependencies provide DAG orchestration. Retry logic handles Spot interruptions transparently. S3 multipart optimizes throughput.

q: Your organization needs to process streaming data: 100,000 events/second, complex transformations, analytics, and long-term storage. Sub-5-second end-to-end latency. How to architect?
options: Kinesis Data Streams for ingestion with auto-scaling, Kinesis Data Analytics for real-time aggregations and anomaly detection, Kinesis Firehose to S3 for archive, Lambda for complex transformations, SNS for alerts
explain: Kinesis handles 100K EPS natively. Analytics provides real-time aggregations. Firehose ensures durability. Lambda allows complex transformations. Architecture designed for sub-5s latency.

q: Your organization runs distributed training for machine learning models across 1000+ GPUs. Models take 10 hours to train. Infrastructure spans multiple AWS regions and on-premises. How to architect?
options: Single EC2 p4d instance with multi-GPU training
Manual distributed training orchestration with AWS Batch
Single on-premises cluster with cloud burst

SageMaker distributed training with Horovod (inter-node communication), custom training containers, multi-region training with EFA (Elastic Fabric Adapter) for low-latency inter-node communication, on-premises training via AWS Outposts, spot instances for 70% cost savings
explain: SageMaker distributed training manages complex orchestration. EFA provides <5us latency for inter-node communication. Horovod scales gradient computation. Spot instances reduce 10-hour training cost 70%.

q: Your organization requires HIPAA-compliant data processing: PII data, encryption everywhere, audit trails, access control, 6-year retention. Data includes patient medical records, genetic data. How to architect securely?
options: S3 with server-side encryption, IAM policies

S3 with client-side encryption (asymmetric keys), S3 Object Lock for immutability, VPC endpoints to prevent internet access, CloudTrail logging all API calls, GuardDuty for intrusion detection, Macie for data classification, BareMetalInstance for HSM-based encryption key management, Config compliance rules for HIPAA standard
explain: Client-side encryption + Object Lock + VPC endpoint + GuardDuty + Macie provides HIPAA-grade security. HSM ensures cryptographic key control. Config compliance demonstrates adherence.

q: Your organization implements fraud detection: transaction stream (1M/hour), real-time scoring via ML model, blocking fraudulent transactions <100ms. Models update hourly with new training data. How to architect?
options: Kinesis Data Streams ingests transactions, Lambda invokes SageMaker real-time endpoint (A/B testing with production + canary), Model registry stores models, hourly SageMaker training job updates endpoint, SNS alerts for high fraud scores, DynamoDB caches transaction history for feature engineering
explain: Kinesis provides transaction stream. SageMaker endpoints support A/B testing (production + canary). Model registry tracks versions. Hourly jobs update models. DynamoDB enables feature engineering at scale.

q: Your organization runs a SaaS platform serving 10,000+ customers with multi-tenancy. Tenants have different resource needs, SLAs, data residency. One tenant's infrastructure failure should not impact others. How to architect?
options: RDS Aurora cluster per silo (high-touch tenants) or Shared database with row-level security (standard tenants), separate VPC per security domain (data residency), cross-account isolation for premium tenants, AWS Glue data catalog per tenant for analytics
Single shared database with tenant ID column
explain: Tiered multi-tenancy: premium = separate cluster, standard = shared with RLS. VPCs per data residency domain. Cross-account isolation for highest-security tenants.

q: Your organization implements a serverless data lake: data ingestion (S3), cataloging (Glue), queries (Athena), analytics (QuickSight), security (Lake Formation). 500 users with different access levels. How to architect?
options: S3 data partitioning by date/source, Glue Crawler populates Catalog, Lake Formation permissions control access per user/group/table/column, Athena for SQL queries, QuickSight connected directly to Athena/Redshift Spectrum with RLS, Apache Iceberg for ACID transactions
Manual data management with SQL scripts
Glue crawler → Athena queries → QuickSight dashboards
explain: Lake Formation provides centralized permission management. Iceberg adds ACID transactions to S3 data lake. Athena + QuickSight directly consume catalog. RLS enforces permissions at BI tool level.

q: Your organization manages hybrid infrastructure: AWS + on-premises + edge locations. Need consistent container orchestration, service mesh, observability. 500+ microservices. How to architect?
options: EKS Anywhere for on-premises clusters, managed EKS for AWS, AWS Service Mesh (App Mesh with Envoy proxies), X-Ray for observability, Control Plane managed centrally via AWS APIs, cross-cluster communication via Transit Gateway
Kubernetes manually on-premises, ECS in AWS
explain: EKS Anywhere extends Kubernetes to on-premises. App Mesh provides unified service mesh. X-Ray distributed tracing across hybrid environment. TGW enables cross-cluster networking.

q: Your organization requires real-time anomaly detection across all AWS resources. 500+ accounts, 10,000+ resources. Anomalies must trigger actions <5 seconds. How to architect?
options: GuardDuty for intrusion detection, Security Hub for security findings, Trusted Advisor for infrastructure anomalies, EventBridge aggregates findings from all accounts via delegated admin account, Lambda analyzes patterns via ML model, SNS alerts + Systems Manager auto-remediation, X-Ray traces anomaly causation
explain: GuardDuty + Security Hub + Trusted Advisor provide multi-signal anomaly detection. EventBridge aggregates findings. Lambda ML model analyzes patterns. Systems Manager auto-remediation triggers <5s.

q: Your organization runs a video streaming platform: 100 Tbps peak throughput, global audience, 4K + HDR content. Users expect adaptive bitrate, offline download, <500ms start-up latency. How to architect?
options: AWS Elemental MediaPackage for live encoding + packaging, CloudFront global delivery with Lambda@Edge for bitrate selection, S3 for VOD with Intelligent-Tiering, DynamoDB for user state (playback position, bookmarks), offline download via Glacier Deep Archive with on-demand restoration
explain: MediaPackage handles complex encoding. Lambda@Edge makes real-time bitrate decisions. CloudFront delivers to global audience. DynamoDB tracks user state. Glacier enables archival with on-demand access.

q: Your organization implements complex event processing: 1M events/second, state machines, complex join operations across multiple event streams, 99.99% accuracy, exactly-once semantics. How to architect?
options: Kinesis Data Streams, Kinesis Data Analytics for complex CEP (pattern matching, tumbling/sliding windows), Step Functions for state machine orchestration, DynamoDB for exactly-once idempotency tracking, Firehose to S3 for audit trail, X-Ray for end-to-end tracing
explain: Kinesis Analytics provides CEP language for pattern matching. Step Functions orchestrates workflows. DynamoDB idempotency ensures exactly-once. Firehose provides immutable audit trail.

q: Your organization manages edge computing: 500+ edge locations with local GPU processing, cloud training, global model serving. Latency SLA: <10ms at edge. How to architect?
options: Inference via Kinesis at cloud only

AWS IoT Greengrass for edge deployment, SageMaker Neo compilation for edge runtime optimization, local inference with <10ms latency, cloud SageMaker for retraining, fleet-wide model updates via IoT Jobs, S3 bucket for model versioning
explain: IoT Greengrass enables local inference <10ms. SageMaker Neo optimizes models for edge hardware. IoT Jobs orchestrate fleet-wide updates. S3 model versioning provides rollback capability.

q: Your organization implements a serverless web application: API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB + S3. Application growth expectation: 10x traffic in 6 months. How to architect for elastic scaling?
options: Lambda with provisioned concurrency (eliminates cold start), DynamoDB on-demand billing (auto-scales), CloudFront caching with TTL optimization, S3 Transfer Acceleration for global uploads, auto-scaling Lambda provisioned concurrency based on predicted load via CloudWatch anomaly detection
Fixed Lambda memory, provisioned DynamoDB capacity
explain: Provisioned concurrency + on-demand DynamoDB auto-scales elastically. CloudFront caching reduces backend load. CloudWatch anomaly detection predicts load spikes. Saves costs while handling 10x growth.

q: Your organization requires a search platform: 100TB index, 1M queries/second, sub-100ms search latency, faceted search, spelling correction. How to architect?
options: Amazon OpenSearch on dedicated hardware, UltraWarm storage for historical data, cross-cluster replication for geo-distribution, k-NN search plugin for ML-based recommendations, Query DSL with SpellChecker plugin, CloudFront in front for query result caching
explain: OpenSearch handles 1M QPS natively. UltraWarm stores historical data cost-effectively. Cross-cluster replication provides geo-distribution. k-NN enables ML search.

q: Your organization runs a multi-protocol API (REST, GraphQL, gRPC, MQTT). Clients have varying latency tolerances (10ms to 5s). Single endpoint. How to architect?
options: Network Load Balancer with TLS SNI routing, API Gateway for REST (transformed by Lambda@Edge), AppSync for GraphQL (with DataSource connectors), gRPC service via target group (ALB supports HTTP/2), MQTT via IoT Core, unified auth via Cognito + Lambda authorizer
explain: NLB with SNI routes based on certificate/protocol. Each protocol uses native AWS service (AppSync for GraphQL, IoT Core for MQTT). Single endpoint via unified auth layer.

q: Your organization implements ML Ops pipeline: continuous training (daily), model validation, A/B testing in production, model registry, automated rollback. 100+ models in production. How to architect?
options: SageMaker Pipelines for orchestration (data validation, training, model evaluation), Model Registry for versioning, SageMaker Feature Store for feature management, Lambda for A/B testing experiment orchestration, CloudWatch alarms trigger automatic rollback if model metrics degrade, CodePipeline stages for approval gates
explain: Pipelines automate daily retraining. Feature Store provides consistent features. A/B testing Lambda routes traffic intelligently. Alarms trigger auto-rollback. CodePipeline approval gates ensure governance.

q: Your organization implements autonomous systems: vehicles, drones, robots. Real-time processing at edge, cloud training, fleet management. 10,000+ autonomous units. How to architect?
options: AWS IoT Greengrass Core on vehicles for local inference, Fleet Provisioning for onboarding, AWS IoT Device Management for fleet control, cloud DynamoDB for global state (location, telemetry), SageMaker for model updates, Systems Manager for OTA deployment, S3 for sensor data archive
explain: Greengrass Core enables local autonomous decision-making. Fleet Provisioning auto-scales to 10K units. Device Management provides control plane. SageMaker enables continuous model improvements.

q: Your organization operates 5,000+ servers on-premises (VM-based). Legacy business applications, some with 20-year-old code. 2-year migration window. How to prioritize and execute?
options: AWS Cloud Adoption Framework: assess (dependency mapping, 6Rs classification), prioritize (quick wins + business value), migrate waves (Rehost/Replatform 70%, Refactor 20%, Retire 10%), AWS MGN for rehost, AWS DMS for databases
Rehost all servers to EC2 immediately
explain: CAF provides structured approach. 6Rs classification enables right-sizing per app. MGN handles large-scale rehost. DMS automates database migration. Phased approach minimizes risk.

q: Your organization has monolithic Java application (10M lines of code, 200 engineers, 5-year release cycle). Needs to become microservices for faster innovation (2-week releases). How to migrate?
options: Strangler Fig pattern: API facade in front of monolith, new features as microservices, gradually replace monolith components, API Gateway routes traffic, old components fall away over 2 years, no big-bang rewrite
explain: Strangler Fig avoids big-bang risk. API facade enables gradual replacement. Traffic routing controlled. Components retire naturally as new versions ship.

q: Your organization migrates 500+ databases from on-premises Oracle (multi-terabyte) to AWS. Different apps have different downtime tolerances (0 min, 1 hr, 1 day). How to execute at scale?
options: AWS Schema Conversion Tool (auto-convert schema), DMS with continuous replication (weeks before cutover), database-specific cutover strategy (0-downtime via Aurora read replica + switchover, 1hr via reboot, 1day via batch), validation post-cutover, rollback plan per DB
AWS DMS with full-load + CDC for all databases
Lift-and-shift via Oracle Cloud VM import
explain: SCT handles schema conversion. DMS continuous replication enables zero-downtime for 0-min apps. Flexible cutover strategy per app tolerance. Validation ensures data integrity.

q: Your organization has 10,000+ traditional IT management servers (configuration, patch, deploy, monitor). Cloud-native apps emerging. Need unified management across hybrid. How to architect?
options: Keep separate legacy and cloud tooling

AWS Systems Manager for unified management: Patch Manager for patching (hybrid agents), Session Manager for console access, Automation documents for runbooks, Ops Center for incident management, CloudWatch agent for monitoring everywhere, IAM for access control
Custom management tool across all servers
explain: Systems Manager bridges hybrid management. Patch Manager + Session Manager provide control. Automation documents codify runbooks. CloudWatch centralizes observability.

q: Your organization migrates Windows Server workloads (Active Directory, SQL Server, Exchange, SharePoint). 200+ servers, complex interdependencies. Minimizes business disruption. How to migrate?
options: Replatform to cloud-native (eliminate AD, use Cognito, eliminate SQL Server, use RDS/Aurora)

AWS MGN (Application Migration Service) for Windows servers, AWS DataSync for file migrations, AWS Database Migration Service for SQL Server, AWS Directory Service for AD, migration in waves per application stack, parallel prod validation before cutover
explain: MGN handles Windows rehost natively. DataSync migrates files efficiently. Database Migration Service handles SQL Server. Directory Service provides AD parity.

q: Your organization has containerized microservices running on-premises (Docker, Kubernetes). Needs AWS deployment without managing Kubernetes. How to architect?
options: ECS Fargate for containers (no Kubernetes), Service discovery via CloudMap, ALB for load balancing, auto-scaling via target tracking, CloudWatch logs for observability, AWS App Mesh for service mesh if needed
explain: Fargate eliminates Kubernetes operational burden. CloudMap provides service discovery. Auto-scaling handles demand elasticity. Fargate best for teams without Kubernetes expertise.

q: Your organization runs SAP ERP system on-premises (300+ users, 2TB database, mission-critical 24/7). Migration must have <5 min RTO, zero data loss. How to migrate?
options: AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) for compute + AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) continuous replication for SAP database, parallel environment testing, Route 53 health check triggers automatic DNS failover to AWS, rollback procedure pre-tested, cutover during maintenance window <5 min
Keep on-premises, use AWS for disaster recovery only
SAP HANA on AWS with data import
explain: MGN replicates SAP compute. DMS continuous replication ensures database consistency. Route 53 health check triggers automatic failover. Pre-tested rollback ensures safety.

q: Your organization has Salesforce on-premises customization layer (1M lines of Apex code, custom integrations). Needs to run on Salesforce cloud. How to migrate?
options: Salesforce Functions (serverless compute on SFDC platform), AWS Lambda for complex logic (via Salesforce outbound events), Salesforce DataCloud for unified data, AWS integration via VPC endpoints, Apex code gradually refactored to Functions
explain: Salesforce Functions runs serverless in SFDC. Lambda handles complex AWS-specific logic. DataCloud provides unified view. Gradual refactoring avoids big-bang risk.

q: Your organization operates data warehouse (Teradata, 100TB, 2,000+ reports). Migration to AWS required. Some reports run nightly, some real-time dashboards. How to migrate?
options: AWS Glue ETL scripts replace Teradata ETL, Redshift DW for OLAP (nightly reports), Amazon QuickSight for real-time dashboards (Athena + S3 for ad-hoc), S3 acts as data lake, graduated license cost from Teradata
explain: Glue provides ETL automation. Redshift handles large OLAP workloads. Athena enables ad-hoc queries. QuickSight provides BI. Gradual migration by workload.

q: Your organization operates mainframe legacy billing system (CICS, 40 years old). Needs cloud migration while supporting legacy interfaces (batch files, async messages, FTP). How to architect?
options: Run mainframe in AWS via migration license

AWS Mainframe Modernization: use Automated migration tool to convert COBOL to Java, containerize into ECS, maintain legacy interfaces via API Gateway + Lambda transformers, API Gateway + ALB for FTP protocol support via custom handlers
explain: AWS Mainframe Modernization auto-converts COBOL. ECS containers replace mainframe compute. Lambda transformers bridge legacy interfaces. Maintains interfaces while modernizing internally.

q: Your organization has legacy LAMP stack websites (PHP, MySQL, Apache). 50+ websites, mix of in-house and third-party code. How to migrate efficiently?
options: Elastic Beanstalk for PHP apps (auto-scaling, zero-downtime deployments), RDS Aurora MySQL for databases, Route 53 routing per app, S3 + CloudFront for static assets, DMS for MySQL migration, database parameter store for per-app config
Migrate one website at a time manually
explain: Beanstalk handles PHP scaling + deployments. RDS Aurora provides MySQL compatibility. Route 53 enables per-app routing. S3+CloudFront optimizes static content. DMS automates database migration.

q: Your organization operates NoSQL database (MongoDB) on-premises with 500GB data, 10K documents/sec throughput. Needs AWS migration. Some operations require transactions (ACID). How to migrate?

q: Your organization runs Apache Spark analytics jobs on-premises cluster (500 nodes, 10TB working set). Jobs run nightly (6 hour batches). How to migrate to AWS cost-effectively?
options: AWS Glue (serverless Spark) replaces cluster, auto-scales per job, scales 0→500 cores elastically, S3 acts as data lake, provisioned capacity for baseline, Spot for variable capacity, saves 70% vs on-prem cluster amortization

q: Your organization has complex on-premises network (site-to-site VPN, ExpressRoute, multiple data centers). Cloud migration requires hybrid networking. How to design migration?

q: Your organization has geographically distributed offices with local on-premises servers, each with local storage. Needs consolidation to AWS. Local latency requirements must be met. How to migrate?

q: Your organization operates Microsoft Exchange (email/calendar) on-premises (50,000 users). Migration to O365 needed without business disruption. How to migrate?

q: Your organization runs complex build/test/release pipeline for software product (1000+ daily builds, 50+ AWS accounts for test environments). Pipeline manually orchestrated. Needs AWS-native automation. How to architect?

explain: CodePipeline orchestrates multi-stage build. CodeBuild scales test parallelization. Cross-account roles enable 50 test environments. CloudFormation auto-provisions/destroys test env. Cost-optimized via teardown.

q: Your organization has 100+ third-party SaaS integrations (Salesforce, ServiceNow, HubSpot, etc.) in legacy system. Cloud migration requires maintaining integrations. How to architect?

Keep OIM forever, cloud-only apps use separate identity

Run on mainframe emulation in AWS
